data.title: SpaceX showed investors AI device prototype ahead of IPO: report
data.description:
• Ahead of last month's historic initial public offering, SpaceX (SPCX) showed investors  a prototype of a device for how people could interact and use artificial intelligence, The Wall Street Journal reported. 
  • The device, which is believed to run on Qualcomm's (QCOM) Snapdragon processors, is thinner than an Apple (AAPL) iPhone, the news outlet added, citing people familiar with the matter. It also used a new operating system and integrated AI tech from xAI, some of the people added.
  • SpaceX and Qualcomm did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Sphere will aim for the midnight crowd with Rocky Horror Show in Las Vegas
data.description:
Sphere Entertainment (SPHR) extended its strong 2026 rally over the last month after announcing that The Rocky Horror Picture Show at Sphere will be a major show at its Las Vegas venue in 2027. 

The Rocky Horror Picture Show franchise is a cult musical-comedy-horror universe that began as the stage musical The Rocky Horror Show in 1973 and the landmark 1975 film adaptation directed by Jim Sharman. Centered on the misadventures of naïve couple Brad and Janet in the castle of pansexual alien scientist Dr. Frank-N-Furter, it parodies mid-20th-century sci-fi and horror B-movies with glam-rock style and camp humor.

The franchise includes the original stage show, the film, the 1981 movie sequel Shock Treatment, plus numerous revivals, tours, and a 2016 TV remake. Disney (DIS) licensed the Rocky Horror rights to Sphere (SPHR) for the upcoming show.

Susquehanna analyst Joseph Stauff thinks Rocky Horror adds unique IP to its content portfolio that is likely to be targeted for later day-part start times vs. the earlier start times for its more global mass appeal of Wizard of Oz. 

"Importantly, we estimate this will be additive to the total number of original content shows with new start times likely to be in the ~10 p.m.-12 a.m. window. Currently, Wizard of Oz is running ~17 shows per week (at ~85% sell-through), and we raised our 2028 estimates assuming only about three more shows per week at 75% sell-through and ~67% contribution margin, given the more niche offering," highlighted Stauff.

Shares of Sphere Entertainment (SPHR) cooled off on Wednesday with a 2.1% swing lower but are still up more than 75% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: European regulator asks for third-party input in review of Electronic Arts' $55B takeover
data.description:
The European Commission is asking for third-party input as it reviews a consortium led by Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund's planned $55 billion purchase of videogame maker Electronic Arts (EA). 

The Commission is inviting third parties to submit their observations on the proposed concentration to the regulator, according to a filing on Wednesday. The authority is asking for observations to reach the authority no later than 10 days following the date of the publication.

The buyer group filed with the European Commission last month, and the authority has until July 22 to decide to approve the transaction or move it into a more in-depth probe, according to a filing on the regulator's website. The acquisition will also see scrutiny under the European Union's foreign subsidy rules.

Private equity firm Silver Lake, Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund, known as PIF, and Affinity Partners agreed to acquire Electronic Arts (EA) for $210 per share in cash in late September.

data.title: Most and least shorted utilities stocks with market caps up to $2B as of end-June
data.description:
Within the utilities sector (XLU), short interest at the end of June was spread broadly across the sector, with no single industry emerging as a clear standout or dominating in terms of positioning.

VivoPower (VIVO) recorded the highest short interest among utilities stocks with a market capitalization of up to $2 billion, with short interest at 19.50% of its shares outstanding, followed by Cadiz (CDZI) at 8.38%, reflecting elevated bearish positioning in both stocks.

At the other end of the spectrum, AleAnna (ANNA) and Montauk Renewables (MNTK) were among the least shorted utilities stocks, with short interest of 0.68% and 0.82%, respectively.

The five most shorted utilities stocks with market capitalizations of up to $2 billion (as a percentage of shares outstanding) are:

  • VivoPower (VIVO) — 19.50% short interest
  • Cadiz (CDZI) — 8.38%
  • Hallador Energy Company (HNRG) — 5.66%
  • XPLR Infrastructure, LP Common Units (XIFR) — 5.14%
  • Spruce Power Holding (SPRU) — 4.50%

The five least shorted utilities stocks with market capitalizations of up to $2 billion (as a percentage of shares outstanding) are:

  • Global Water Resources (GWRS) — 1.23% short interest
  • Artesian Resources (ARTNA) — 1%
  • RGC Resources (RGCO) — 0.90%
  • Montauk Renewables (MNTK) — 0.82%
  • AleAnna (ANNA) — 0.68%

data.title: Four stock-moving events to watch as FDA revives AdComs
data.description:
The Advisory Committee meetings, a key regulatory step in U.S. drug and MedTech approvals, are making a comeback as the Food and Drug Administration seeks to revisit several controversial decisions made under the former commissioner, Marty Makary.

This article explores four AdCom-related stock-moving events investors will be looking for over the coming weeks as the FDA revives the long-held tradition that was put on the back burner under the previous administration.

The meetings, in which a group of independent experts votes on whether to recommend products under FDA review, are usually convened, especially when the regulator is facing difficult approval decisions. While the advisory committees issue non-binding recommendations, the agency typically follows them before issuing final approvals.

Under Makary, who departed the FDA in May following a series of highly contentious decisions, they were out of vogue, with one former senior agency official arguing in March that the gatherings were “costly,” "slow," and used “pomp and flash instead of scientific truth.”

With a new set of officials at the helm, the agency is putting a renewed focus on the long-standing practice. “We will see more advisory committees. "Period," FDA's acting chief of staff Lowell Zeta said at an annual gathering of biotech industry leaders in San Diego, California, last month.

Drug developers Replimune (REPL) and Capricor Therapeutics (CAPR) are both facing newly scheduled expert reviews in relation to their lead drugs, which were rejected under Makary.

A federal notice indicated last week that the FDA’s Cellular, Tissue, and Gene Therapies Advisory Committee will meet on July 29 to discuss Capricor’s (CAPR) Biologics License Application for its cell therapy Deramiocel targeted at Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

Replimune (REPL) said last week that an FDA advisory committee is scheduled to meet in late July to review its lead drug, RP1, targeted at melanoma, in combination with nivolumab, a cancer therapy marketed by Bristol Myers (BMY) as Opdivo.

Moderna (MRNA) has already won unanimous backing of the FDA's Vaccines and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee for its flu vaccine, mRNA-1010, which is currently under FDA review with a target action date of August 5.

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) is expected to benefit from an advisory committee meeting scheduled for this month to recommend whether to allow compounding pharmacies to manufacture certain peptides. The telehealth firm, which markets compounding drugs, acquired a peptide facility in California last year.

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) is poised to capture a fifth of the telehealth peptide market, expected to reach $2.2B in 2027, according to Leerink Partners. The projection is based on sales estimates for the seven peptides the FDA’s pharmacy compounding advisory committee is set to review on July 23 and 24.

However, the brokerage stopped short of revising earnings estimates and its Market Perform rating on the stock, noting several unknowns, including uncertainty over the final FDA decision on peptides under review.

data.title: News Corp sells moving marketplace to National Holding Co
data.description:
Shares of News Corp (NWSA) are higher Wednesday after the company sold its online moving marketplace subsidiary Moving.com and MoveAI to National Holding Company.

Moving.com is a resource for connecting consumers to professional moving companies, while MoveAI uses artificial intelligence to streamline the search by allowing users to share move details and to receive recommendations and quotes.

National Holding Company is the parent of National Van Lines, a full-service moving and relocation company, operating across all 50 U.S. states and 150 countries.

"By combining Moving.com’s and MoveAI's technology and our decades of operational expertise, we're creating a stronger experience for consumers while generating new opportunities for movers and industry partners," said Tim Helenthal, CEO of National Holding Company.

Terms of the acquisition were not disclosed.

News Corp (NWSA) is up nearly 5%, piercing resistance at the 50- and 200-day moving averages.

data.title: Nonfarm payroll growth is seen slowing but staying 'relatively strong' in June
data.description:
The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics will issue its June Employment Situation report on Thursday instead of the usual Friday, due to observance of the Independence Day holiday. While the Federal Reserve appears to be more focused on inflation than the employment rate recently, the policymakers will still scour the jobs report for signs of economic growth or warnings of weakness.

The U.S. economy is expected to add 114K jobs in June, down from the 172K estimated in May, according to the average estimate of economists. The June report comes after U.S. nonfarm payrolls showed surprisingly strong growth in the past three months, leading many economists to believe the labor market has stabilized.

The unemployment rate is expected to remain at 4.3%. 

The state of the labor market is important because it makes up half of the Federal Reserve's dual mandate — maximum employment and price stability. Its decisions affect the interest rates consumers pay on mortgages and credit card debt and businesses pay on loans and debt to fund expansion.

Nicole Bachaud, a labor economist at jobs site ZipRecruiter, expects a "relatively strong report" with the economy adding 120K-150K jobs in June and unemployment remaining stable.

Major labor trends established earlier this year are expected to continue. Those include labor force participation continuing to fall, job growth broadening across sectors, and employers adjusting to last year's elevated macro uncertainty, she said.

 "We're seeing a lot of renewed resilience from employers, especially getting into the summer season," Bachaud said in an interview with Seeking Alpha. "We've seen a big bump in leisure and hospitality employment for the past couple of months." 

In addition, ZipRecruiter is noticing an increase in summer job postings and temporary positions. "There is this emphasis on this summer of performing quite well, and we've seen the data leading up to that point," she added.

The number of seasonal temporary jobs looks strong for the summer, but in looking ahead, Bachaud looks at temporary services help. "There has been a slight increase in temporary help service employment across the economy, which usually is a sign that things are going to continue to grow," she said.

While almost all of last year's employment growth came from the health care sector, this year other sectors are also contributing. Bachaud points to skilled trades, construction (fueled by the AI infrastructure boom), leisure and hospitality, and business and professional services as strengthening sectors.

"We've really seen this broadening of the labor market away from a single industry dominance and towards more distributed growth," she said.

With the labor market strengthening, the declining labor force participation rate could signal labor market tightening ahead. "That could be putting some pressure on the market as we continue to see job growth happening and fewer people available in the market to work those jobs," Bachaud said.

A factor that could offset the waning participation rate is a lag in employers hiring. ZipRecruiter is seeing hesitation in businesses hiring for open positions. Job openings have increased for the past two months, but the hiring rate has stayed low, she said. Part of it may be related to expectations of increased productivity related to the adoption of artificial intelligence. 

"People who are working in AI-specific fields and roles are seeing more job offers and activity from employers, but they're also seeing more offers being rescinded," as potential employers rethink their labor needs, Bachaud said.

The BLS's Employment Situation report will also provide fresh data on workers' wages. The ZipRecruiter economist highlighted the trend that wage growth is not keeping up with inflation, which could impact household spending.

Average hourly earnings are expected to rise 0.3% M/M, according to the consensus estimate, unchanged from the 0.3% rate it logged in the prior month. On a Y/Y basis, that's anticipated to be 3.5%, accelerating a tick from 3.4% in May. By comparison, headline PCE inflation rose to 4.1% in May.

"Inflation is the thing that could delay growth from continuing at the same pace," Bachaud said.

That has implications for Fed policy. "The expectation that there are probably going to be rate hikes later this year is in line with a stabilized labor market and an increasing level of inflation. Those things are expected now and kind of baked in."

Not everyone expects June's numbers to sustain the strength seen in previous months. Citi's Veronica Clark expects the U.S. economy to add only about 25K jobs due to a low hiring rate, a drop in small business hiring plans, declining Indeed.com job postings, and rising initial jobless claims during the June reference period.

"A number of other weaker data points – most importantly rising initial and continuing jobless claims – lead us to think that strength in payroll data is not a sign of sustainably stronger demand for workers," Clark wrote in a note to clients.

data.title: Tarsus Pharmaceuticals falls amid Culper Research short call
data.description:
• Tarsus Pharmaceuticals (TARS) dropped 7.8% amid a short report from Culper Research.
  • Culper Research disclosed that it's short shares of Tarsus (TARS).
  • Tarsus didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's email request for comment.
  • Tarsus (TARS) has a short interest of 11.6%.

data.title: Apple could face $52M fine in Russia over alleged bias against local apps: report
data.description:
Russia's Federal Antimonopoly Service urged Apple (AAPL) to address what ​it described as discriminatory ⁠practices against Russian ​search engines and ​software, Reuters reported.

The antimonopoly watchdog said the iPhone maker must ensure Russian ​software, including ​search engines and messenger Max, ‌is ⁠pre-installed on its devices, the report added.

If Apple fails to remedy ​the violations ​by ⁠July 15, it could face ​a fine ​of ⁠up to 4B roubles (about $51.6M), the report noted.

Apple did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Apple had officially halted all product sales and exports to Russia in March 2022.

data.title: OTTR, OKLO draw highest short interest in utilities sector; BIP and RNW see lowest short interest
data.description:
Within the utilities sector, short sellers remain heavily concentrated in electric utilities, with several electricity producers and multi-utility companies ranking among the most heavily shorted stocks in the market at the end of June.

Otter Tail (OTTR) and Oklo (OKLO) recorded the highest short interest among utilities stocks with a market capitalization of over $2B.

The utilities sector (XLU) comprises companies involved in providing services like electricity, natural gas, and water, among others.

Otter Tail (OTTR) was the most shorted stock in the utilities sector, with short interest at 15.64% of its shares outstanding, followed by Oklo (OKLO) at 12.21%, reflecting elevated bearish positioning in both stocks.

Otter Tail (OTTR) was rated Hold by Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings and Wall Street analysts.

Oklo’s shares have slipped nearly 25% year to date, underperforming the benchmark S&P 500, which has gained over 9.5% in the same period. Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings are bearish, rating OKLO a Hold. On the other hand, Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha are bullish, rating it a Buy.

At the other end of the spectrum, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) and ReNew Energy Global (RNW) were among the least shorted utilities stocks, with short interest of 0.19% and 1.22%, respectively.

The top five most shorted utilities stocks with a market capitalization above $2B (as a percentage of shares outstanding) are:

  • Otter Tail (OTTR) — 15.64% short interest
  • Oklo (OKLO) — 12.21%
  • H2O America (HTO) — 11.98%
  • Black Hills (BKH) — 11.32%
  • Alliant Energy (LNT) — 9.90%

The five least shorted utilities stocks with a market capitalization above $2B are:

  • PG&E Corporation (PCG) — 1.84% short interest
  • Essential Utilities (WTRG) — 1.66%
  • Sempra (SRE) — 1.60%
  • ReNew Energy Global (RNW) — 1.22%
  • Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) — 0.19%
